summary: Fix the semantic misalignment between the GitHub-uploaded OpenClaw skill line and Hermes skill line. The target is contract parity, not identical internal code. Server Codex must align both skill repos so they present the same Community-facing semantics while keeping framework-specific adapter logic separate.

in_scope: Audit the current GitHub-published OpenClaw skill line and Hermes skill line for semantic drift.
Fix both repos so their Community-facing contract is semantically aligned.
Keep framework-specific implementation differences only where adapter-level differences are necessary.
Update tests and validation so semantic parity is explicitly proven.
Push the fixes back to the correct GitHub skill repos.
out_of_scope: Forcing OpenClaw and Hermes to use identical internal code paths.
Treating template repo openclaw-for-community as the skill-body release repo.

observed_repo_state_20260416.repo_rule: verify the real remote names before pushing; do not rely on stale repo naming assumptions

design_conclusion: OpenClaw and Hermes do not need identical internal code, but they do need the same Community-facing contract.
The correct principle is contract parity and adapter separation.
Do not allow contract drift just because the frameworks are different.
Do not require code-shape sameness where framework-specific runtime or config surfaces differ.
If a framework difference exists, solve it inside the skill or adapter layer without changing community engineering.
semantic_parity_requirements: The meaning of readiness must align across OpenClaw and Hermes.
The meaning of model-config inheritance must align across OpenClaw and Hermes.
The meaning of fail-closed behavior when formal model config is missing must align across OpenClaw and Hermes.
The meaning of onboarding success must align across OpenClaw and Hermes.
The meaning of identity, active webhook subscription, session or sync, recoverable local state, usable send route, and canonical effect must align across OpenClaw and Hermes.

User-visible commands or operations with the same purpose must preserve equivalent semantics and config meaning across both lines, unless a framework-specific rename is unavoidable and clearly documented.

must_do: Audit both skill repos against the same semantic contract, not against superficial code similarity.
Produce an explicit parity checklist or matrix before finalizing.
Push OpenClaw fixes only to the real OpenClaw skill repo.
Push Hermes fixes only to the Hermes skill repo.
must_not_do: Do not modify myproject or Community server to hide skill-side drift.
Do not silently drop functionality on one side in order to fake parity.
Do not count syntax-only success as semantic alignment.
Do not stop after finding drift; repair it and rerun the exact failed checks.

execution_order: Verify the real current GitHub remotes and local working copies for both skill lines.
Build a semantic parity matrix comparing OpenClaw and Hermes.
Identify every real contract drift item.
Patch OpenClaw and Hermes skill repos as needed to close the drift.
Run repo-local tests and any targeted semantic validation checks.
Push the fixes to the correct GitHub repos.
Return exact repo URLs, branches, commit SHAs, changed files, parity summary, and validation evidence.
minimum_acceptance: A written semantic parity summary exists for OpenClaw and Hermes.
The identified semantic drift items are actually repaired, not only documented.
Repo-local tests are green for both skill lines.
The final report explains which semantics were previously misaligned and how each one was aligned.
OpenClaw fixes are pushed to the correct OpenClaw skill repo.
Hermes fixes are pushed to the correct Hermes skill repo.
deliverables: Repo URLs, branches, final commit SHAs, and changed-file lists.
If blocked, the first real blocker with direct evidence.
